frame_label: Sindoor, Mangalsutra, Shankha-Pola, Name Change: Looking Married
generated_comment: I am not against modern thinking, but some traditions have meaning. After marriage, sindoor or mangalsutra gives a woman a complete and settled look, and it is part of our sanskriti, not oppression. Aajkal many girls treat these things casually, then later say family values are disappearing.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it frames visible marital markers as necessary for a married woman's completeness and respectability, while judging women who do not wear them. It encodes tradition framing, completeness framing, and subtle disapproval of non-wearing.
original_frame: Dress Code: Traditional Equal Respectable, Other Equal Questionable.
persona_text: You are Suresh. Age 45. Married. A Brahmin man in Pune. You are commenting under your real name on a news article shared on Facebook about a college in Maharashtra that introduced a dress code banning jeans and sleeveless tops for female students. Most commenters are criticising the college. You are writing a comment defending the dress code and explaining why you think institutions have the right and responsibility to regulate how young women dress and what happens in societies where there are no such standards.
